sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage:
An immunology clinic compares four adverse immune reactions. Patient A develops hives and wheezing minutes after peanut exposure. Patient B develops hemolytic anemia after a drug binds red blood cell surfaces. Patient C develops serum sickness with immune-complex deposition after receiving an animal-derived antitoxin. Patient D develops a delayed rash 48 hours after poison ivy exposure.

Patient | Timing | Main mediator | Pattern
A | minutes | IgE/mast cells | immediate
B | hours-days | IgG/complement | cell surface
C | days | immune complexes | deposition
D | 48-72 h | T cells | delayed

The physician reviews hypersensitivity types I-IV.
questions.stem: Patient A's reaction is most consistent with:

explanations.A: Type II involves antibodies against cell-surface or matrix antigens, such as drug-coated red cells.
explanations.B: Correct. Immediate hives and wheezing after allergen exposure reflect IgE-mediated mast-cell degranulation, the hallmark of Type I hypersensitivity.
explanations.C: Type III involves immune-complex deposition and complement activation over days.
explanations.D: Type IV is T-cell mediated and delayed, usually appearing after 48-72 hours.

questions.stem: Patient B's hemolytic anemia is Type II because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: antibodies target antigens on cell surfaces leading to complement or phagocyte-mediated damage

choices.label: B
choices.text: IgE binds mast cells after peanut exposure

choices.label: C
choices.text: immune complexes deposit in vessel walls only

explanations.A: Correct. Drug-modified red cell surfaces can become targets for IgG or IgM, activating complement or phagocytosis. That cell-surface antibody pattern is Type II.
explanations.B: IgE mast-cell activation describes Patient A and Type I allergy.
explanations.C: Immune complex deposition describes Type III reactions such as serum sickness.
explanations.D: Delayed T-cell responses describe Type IV, not antibody-coated red cells.

questions.stem: Serum sickness in Patient C is best classified as:

explanations.A: Type II targets fixed antigens on cell surfaces, not circulating complexes depositing in tissues.
explanations.B: Type I would be rapid IgE-mediated mast-cell activation, not delayed immune-complex deposition.
explanations.C: Correct. Serum sickness occurs when soluble antigen-antibody complexes deposit in tissues and activate complement. That immune-complex mechanism is Type III.
explanations.D: Type IV is mediated by T cells rather than immune complexes.

questions.stem: Poison ivy delayed rash is Type IV because it is mediated mainly by:

explanations.A: Immune complexes define Type III, not classic poison ivy contact dermatitis.
explanations.B: IgE cross-linking produces immediate Type I reactions such as anaphylaxis or urticaria.
explanations.C: Histamine contributes to some inflammation but does not define the delayed T-cell mechanism.
explanations.D: Correct. Type IV hypersensitivity is delayed and T-cell mediated. Poison ivy haptens modify skin proteins, leading to T-cell recognition and inflammation after 48-72 hours.

questions.stem: Which treatment most directly reverses life-threatening Type I anaphylaxis acutely?

explanations.A: Correct. Epinephrine rapidly bronchodilates, vasoconstricts, and supports blood pressure during anaphylaxis. It addresses the acute physiologic consequences of mast-cell mediator release.
explanations.B: A booster vaccine builds adaptive memory over time and does not treat acute anaphylaxis.
explanations.C: Patch testing can diagnose delayed contact hypersensitivity but does not reverse airway compromise.
explanations.D: Oral iron treats iron deficiency, not allergic shock.

questions.passage: A vaccine trial compares an inactivated viral vaccine with an mRNA vaccine encoding a surface antigen. Both increase neutralizing antibodies after two doses. Memory B cells and T cells are measured six months later. A booster increases antibody titers rapidly, consistent with immunologic memory. The researchers distinguish active immunity from passive antibody transfer and note that adjuvants enhance innate immune activation to improve adaptive responses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the host generates its own adaptive response and memory

explanations.A: Passive immunity is transfer of preformed antibodies, such as immune globulin, not vaccination's main mechanism.
explanations.B: Correct. Active immunity means the recipient's immune system responds to antigen, generating effector cells and memory B/T cells. Vaccines are designed to induce that response.
explanations.C: Lymphocytes are central to adaptive vaccine responses.
explanations.D: Antigen presentation is required to activate T cells and support robust antibody responses.

questions.stem: Why does a booster produce a rapid antibody rise?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Memory B cells respond faster than naïve B cells

explanations.A: Boosters stimulate memory cells; they do not delete plasma cells as the desired effect.
explanations.B: Antibodies do not become innate receptors; they are adaptive immune proteins.
explanations.C: Correct. Memory B cells generated by earlier doses require less activation time and can rapidly differentiate into antibody-secreting cells. That produces a faster, larger secondary response.
explanations.D: Clonal expansion is part of the booster response, not something boosters prevent.

questions.stem: What is the role of an adjuvant?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Replace all antigen in the vaccine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhance innate activation to strengthen adaptive immunity

explanations.A: Adjuvants generally improve antigen presentation and immune activation rather than preventing it.
explanations.B: Adjuvants support responses to antigen; they do not replace the antigenic target entirely.
explanations.C: Passive antibodies are transferred directly and do not require an adjuvant to induce host memory.
explanations.D: Correct. Adjuvants activate innate immune pathways and improve costimulation/cytokine signals, making adaptive responses to the antigen stronger and more durable.

choices.label: A
choices.text: killing infected cells by MHC I recognition directly

choices.label: B
choices.text: blocking pathogen attachment or entry into host cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: digesting antigen in lysosomes before binding

explanations.A: MHC I recognition of infected cells is performed by cytotoxic T cells, not antibodies directly.
explanations.B: Correct. Neutralizing antibodies bind pathogen surface structures and prevent receptor binding, fusion, or entry. That stops infection before productive intracellular replication.
explanations.C: Antibodies bind extracellular antigen before lysosomal processing; they do not need to digest antigen first.
explanations.D: B cells and T cells are distinct lineages. Antibodies do not transform into memory T cells.

questions.stem: Which feature would best show durable vaccine memory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antigen-specific memory B and T cells detectable months later

explanations.A: Correct. Long-lived memory lymphocytes months after vaccination indicate durable adaptive immunity. A rapid booster response would further support functional memory.
explanations.B: Fever can reflect innate activation but does not prove durable antigen-specific memory.
explanations.C: Immediate antibody disappearance would suggest poor persistence and does not show memory by itself.
explanations.D: No booster response would argue against effective immunologic memory.
